title: Hormones — chemical messengers
examTip: AQA loves the nervous system vs hormonal system comparison — it comes up in almost every B5 paper. Have a fluent, structured answer ready: nervous is fast/short/precise; hormonal is slow/long-lasting/broad. Then know your glands and their hormones by heart.
introTips: You're going to learn about your body's second communication system — slower than nerves but capable of controlling whole-body changes that last for hours.
There are six endocrine glands to know with their key hormones. Learning them as gland + hormone + effect is the most efficient route.
The comparison between the nervous system and the hormonal system is extremely likely to come up — prepare a clear, structured answer.
intro: Your nervous system can send a signal in milliseconds — it's like a text message. But your hormonal system is more like a letter sent through the post: slower to arrive, but once it does, the effect lasts much longer. Hormones control some of the biggest changes in your body — your response to danger, your blood glucose levels, your growth, your reproductive cycle. Understanding which gland makes which hormone and what it does is essential for at least a third of the B5 exam marks.

keyPoints.heading: What hormones are and how they travel
keyPoints.content: Hormones are chemical messengers secreted by endocrine glands directly into the blood. They travel dissolved in the blood plasma to target organs — organs that have specific receptor proteins for that hormone. Only cells with the matching receptor protein will respond to a particular hormone, which is why a hormone travelling through the whole body can affect only specific tissues. Hormones produce effects that are generally slower to start than nervous responses but longer-lasting. The system that produces, regulates, and responds to hormones is called the endocrine system.
writeBullets: Hormones = chemical messengers secreted by endocrine glands into the blood
Transported in blood plasma to target organs throughout the body
Target organs have specific receptor proteins for the hormone
Only cells with matching receptors respond to a particular hormone
Hormonal responses: slower to start, longer-lasting than nervous responses

keyTerms.def: A chemical messenger secreted by an endocrine gland into the blood, which travels to target organs to produce an effect
A gland that secretes hormones directly into the blood (no duct)
The organ that responds to a particular hormone because it has receptor proteins that match the hormone
The system of glands and hormones that regulates body functions through chemical signals

keyPoints.memTip: Hormones travel in BLOOD to TARGET organs. The key distinction from nerves is: hormones use blood (slow, long-lasting, broad); nerves use electrical impulses (fast, short, precise). Fix that contrast and the rest of this topic makes sense.

keyPoints.heading: Nervous vs hormonal control — a comparison
keyPoints.content: AQA regularly asks you to compare the nervous and hormonal systems. The nervous system uses electrical impulses along neurones — responses are very fast (milliseconds), effects are short-lived, and responses are very precise (affecting one specific muscle or gland). The hormonal system uses chemical messengers (hormones) carried in the blood — responses are slower (seconds to minutes), effects are longer-lasting (hours to days), and the response is broader (can affect multiple organs simultaneously). Neither system is 'better' — they suit different purposes: nerves are ideal for rapid reflexes; hormones are ideal for sustained, widespread regulation.
writeBullets: Nervous: electrical impulses, very fast, short-lived, precise (one effector)
Hormonal: chemical messengers in blood, slower, longer-lasting, broader effect
Nervous system: response in milliseconds
Hormonal system: response in seconds to minutes; effects last hours to days
Both systems work together — the hypothalamus links them (controls pituitary)

keyTerms.def: Rapid electrical impulse along a neurone — fast, short-lived, and precise
Chemical signal via blood — slower, longer-lasting, and broader in effect

keyPoints.memTip: Fast-Short-Precise = Nervous. Slow-Long-Broad = Hormonal. FSP vs SLB. Write this table from memory: three rows (speed, duration, precision), two columns (nervous, hormonal). That table answers almost every comparison question.

keyPoints.heading: Key endocrine glands and their hormones
keyPoints.content: The pituitary gland in the brain is the 'master gland' — it secretes multiple hormones that control other endocrine glands. The thyroid gland (in the neck) produces thyroxine, which controls metabolic rate. The adrenal glands (above the kidneys) produce adrenaline, the 'fight or flight' hormone released in stressful situations. The pancreas produces insulin and glucagon, which regulate blood glucose levels. The ovaries produce oestrogen and progesterone (controlling the menstrual cycle). The testes produce testosterone (controlling male secondary sexual characteristics). The hypothalamus in the brain controls the pituitary gland and acts as the link between the nervous and endocrine systems.
writeBullets: Pituitary gland: 'master gland' — controls other endocrine glands
Thyroid gland: produces thyroxine — regulates metabolic rate
Adrenal glands: produce adrenaline — fight or flight response
Pancreas: produces insulin (lowers blood glucose) and glucagon (raises blood glucose)
Ovaries: produce oestrogen and progesterone — menstrual cycle control
Testes: produce testosterone — male secondary sexual characteristics

keyTerms.def: The 'master gland' in the brain that secretes hormones controlling other endocrine glands
Hormone produced by the adrenal glands; prepares the body for 'fight or flight'
Hormone produced by the pancreas; lowers blood glucose by converting glucose to glycogen
Hormone produced by the pancreas; raises blood glucose by converting glycogen back to glucose
Hormone produced by the ovaries; involved in the menstrual cycle and female secondary sexual characteristics

keyPoints.memTip: Learn the glands as a top-to-bottom body scan: brain (pituitary/hypothalamus) → neck (thyroid) → above kidneys (adrenal) → behind stomach (pancreas) → pelvis (ovaries/testes). Each location gets one or two hormones. A body-map approach makes this much easier to recall under pressure.

keyPoints.heading: Adrenaline — the fight or flight hormone
keyPoints.content: When you perceive danger or extreme stress, the adrenal glands release adrenaline into the blood almost instantaneously. Adrenaline prepares your body for rapid physical action: it increases heart rate (to pump more blood to muscles), causes pupils to dilate (to take in more visual information), causes glucose to be released from glycogen stores (to fuel muscles), and diverts blood away from the digestive system towards skeletal muscles. Breathing rate also increases to deliver more oxygen. These responses collectively maximise your ability to fight or run — the 'fight or flight' response. Adrenaline works very quickly for a hormone because the body needs to respond before conscious thought catches up.
writeBullets: Adrenaline released by adrenal glands in response to stress or danger
Increases heart rate — more blood (and oxygen/glucose) to muscles
Pupils dilate — more light enters for better awareness
Glucose released from glycogen stores — more energy for muscles
Blood redirected from digestive system to skeletal muscles
Breathing rate increases — more oxygen to muscles

keyTerms.def: The set of physiological changes triggered by adrenaline to prepare the body for rapid action in response to danger
Glands situated above the kidneys that secrete adrenaline into the blood

keyPoints.memTip: Adrenaline's effects — 'HELP': Heart rate up, Eyes (pupils) dilate, Liver releases glucose, Panting (breathing rate up). Four effects, one acronym, memorised in thirty seconds.

tables.title: Key endocrine glands and their hormones
rows: Master gland — controls other endocrine glands

0.question: How do hormones travel from endocrine glands to target organs?
0.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct — hormones are secreted into the blood and travel dissolved in blood plasma to target organs.
0.wrongFeedback: Hormones travel via the blood — dissolved in blood plasma. Electrical impulses along neurones is the nervous system. The lymphatic and digestive systems are not the route for hormones.

1.question: Compared to the nervous system, how does the hormonal system differ?
options: Hormonal responses are faster and more precise
Hormonal responses are slower, longer-lasting, and have a broader effect
Hormonal responses travel along neurones and are short-lived
Hormonal responses only affect one organ at a time
1.correctFeedback: ✅ Perfect — slower, longer-lasting, and broader effect. That's the three-point contrast AQA is looking for.
1.wrongFeedback: The hormonal system is slower than the nervous system, not faster. Its effects last longer (hours to days vs milliseconds) and can affect multiple organs simultaneously because hormones travel through the blood.

3.question: Which of the following correctly describes an effect of adrenaline?
options: Decreases heart rate to conserve energy for fighting
Causes pupils to constrict to protect the eyes
Increases heart rate and releases glucose from glycogen stores
Diverts blood to the digestive system for energy
3.correctFeedback: ✅ Right — adrenaline increases heart rate (more blood to muscles) and releases glucose from glycogen (more energy). Both are preparation for rapid physical action.
3.wrongFeedback: Adrenaline prepares the body for action: heart rate INCREASES (not decreases), pupils DILATE (not constrict), and blood is diverted AWAY from the digestive system (to muscles). Glucose is released FROM glycogen stores, not the other way round.

commonMistakes: Confusing the nervous and hormonal systems in comparison questions — fix the three-point contrast: nervous = fast/short/precise; hormonal = slow/long/broad. Write it ten times.
Saying 'hormones travel along nerves' — they don't. Hormones travel in the blood. Electrical impulses travel along neurones. These are two completely separate systems.
Getting the gland-hormone pairing wrong — the most common error is assigning insulin to the wrong gland. Insulin and glucagon both come from the pancreas.

Forgetting that only target organs respond to a hormone — examiners sometimes ask why a hormone affects one organ but not another. The answer is receptor proteins: only cells with the matching receptor respond.

Describing all adrenaline effects as slowing down — every adrenaline effect is about INCREASING something (heart rate, glucose availability, pupil size, breathing rate). If you're writing 'decreases', check your answer.
examTips: For comparison questions, give a minimum of two differences between the nervous and hormonal systems. Speed AND duration together are much stronger than just one point.
When naming a gland, always state its hormone AND the hormone's effect — 'the pancreas produces insulin, which lowers blood glucose.' Three parts: gland, hormone, effect.

AQA sometimes gives a scenario (e.g. a student is nervous before an exam) and asks you to explain the hormonal response — name adrenaline, name the adrenal glands, then list effects: increased heart rate, dilated pupils, glucose released.
The hypothalamus links the nervous and endocrine systems by controlling the pituitary gland — if the question asks how the two systems are connected, this is your answer.
revisionCardBullets: Hormones = chemical messengers secreted into blood by endocrine glands → travel to target organs
Target organs respond because they have specific receptor proteins for the hormone
Nervous vs hormonal: nervous = fast/short/precise; hormonal = slow/long-lasting/broad
Pituitary gland = 'master gland' — controls other endocrine glands
Adrenal glands → adrenaline → fight or flight
Adrenaline effects: heart rate up, pupils dilate, glucose released, breathing rate up
Hypothalamus links nervous and endocrine systems via the pituitary gland
